perturbation.original_text: Owner will, in the case of an escape or discharge of products or threat of escape or discharge of same from the applicable vessel into the navigable waters of the United States, promptly undertake such measures as are reasonably necessary or which may be required by applicable laws, rules and regulations to mitigate the resultant pollution damage; provided, however, that Charterer may at its option, and upon notice to Owner and on the conditions hereinafter set forth, undertake such measures.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner will, in the case of an escape or discharge of products or threat of escape or discharge of same from the applicable vessel into any waters, promptly undertake measures that are cost-effective to mitigate the resultant pollution damage; provided, however, that Charterer may not undertake such measures.
perturbation.explanation: The original text references 'navigable waters of the United States,' a defined term under the Clean Water Act. The modified text broadens this to 'any waters' and restricts the Charterer's ability to undertake mitigation measures, conflicting with federal regulations that empower responsible parties and designated responders.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Clean Water Act, specifically provisions related to oil spill response and responsible party obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text expands the scope of waters beyond the legal definition of 'navigable waters' and restricts the charterer from undertaking mitigation measures. The Clean Water Act outlines procedures and responsibilities for oil spill response, including the roles of responsible parties. By preventing the Charterer from undertaking such measures, the modification potentially contradicts the Act's provisions for timely and effective response.

Summary of the Clean Water Act Quick Links 2018 version of CWA from the U.S. Code (233 pp, 1.23 MB) The official text of the CWA continues to be available in the United States Code from the US Government Printing Office 33 U.S.C. §1251 et seq. (1972) The Clean Water Act (CWA) establishes the basic structure for regulating discharges of pollutants into the waters of the United States and regulating quality standards for surface waters. The basis of the CWA was enacted in 1948 and was called the Federal Water Pollution Control Act, but the Act was significantly reorganized and expanded in 1972. "Clean Water Act" became the Act's common name with amendments in 1972. Under the CWA, EPA has implemented pollution control programs such as setting wastewater standards for industry. EPA has also developed national water quality criteria recommendations for pollutants in surface waters. The CWA made it unlawful to discharge any pollutant from a point source into navigable waters, unless a permit was obtained: EPA's National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit program controls discharges. Point sources are discrete conveyances such as pipes or man-made ditches. Individual homes that are connected to a municipal system, use a septic system, or do not have a surface discharge do not need a NPDES permit; Industrial, municipal, and other facilities must obtain permits if their discharges go directly to surface waters. Compliance and Enforcement Clean Water Act Compliance Monitoring : investigations and inspections Water Enforcement History of this Act EPA History: Federal Water Pollution Control Act Amendments of 1972 History of the Clean Water Act Archived EPA History: Clean Water Act More Information The Office of Water (OW) ensures drinking water is safe, and restores and maintains oceans, watersheds, and their aquatic ecosystems to protect human health, support economic and recreational activities, and provide healthy habitat for fish, plants, and wildlife. The EPA Watershed Academy provides training courses on statutes, watershed protection, and other key Clean Water Act resources. perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the scope of affected waters from 'navigable waters of the United States' to 'any waters' while simultaneously restricting the Charterer's ability to undertake mitigation measures. This contradicts the Clean Water Act's intent to ensure effective and timely responses to pollution incidents. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references that the Clean Water Act (CWA) establishes the basic structure for regulating discharges of pollutants into the waters of the United States, and that it was made unlawful to discharge any pollutant from a point source into navigable waters, unless a permit was obtained.

perturbation.original_text: Owner warrants that it will maintain and enforce at all times during the Term of this Agreement a drug and alcohol abuse policy applicable to the vessels which complies in all material respects with the minimum standards promulgated by the U.S. Coast Guard.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner warrants that it will maintain a general policy discouraging drug and alcohol abuse applicable to the vessels; however, the specific enforcement of this policy is at Owner's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates compliance with US Coast Guard drug and alcohol policies, while the modified text makes enforcement discretionary. This creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance with federal maritime regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Coast Guard regulations on drug and alcohol testing for maritime personnel.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the mandatory nature of U.S. Coast Guard regulations regarding drug and alcohol testing and enforcement. 46 CFR § 16.201 et seq. requires specific procedures and consequences for violations. By making enforcement discretionary, the contract creates a situation where the Owner might not fully comply with these federal regulations, leading to potential legal issues.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's commitment to US Coast Guard drug and alcohol policies. The original text mandated compliance with USCG standards, while the changed text makes enforcement discretionary. According to the Law Explanation, 46 CFR § 16.201 et seq. requires specific procedures and consequences for violations. By making enforcement discretionary, the Owner might not fully comply with these federal regulations, leading to potential legal issues. Scraped Law Snippet 1, eCFR :: 46 CFR 16.201, confirms the existence of these regulations. The change introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance, contradicting the legally mandated standards.

perturbation.original_text: Owner shall, before and at commencement of each voyage by any vessel under this Agreement, exercise commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that such vessel is seaworthy and in good operating condition, properly manned, equipped and supplied for the voyage, to ensure that the pipes, pumps and coils tight, staunch, are in good operating condition and fit for the voyage, and to ensure that the tanks and other spaces in which product is to be carried are in good operating condition and fit for the carriage and preservation of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner shall use its best endeavors to ensure that such vessel is adequately maintained. Owner does not warrant the pipes, pumps and coils are tight, staunch, or in good operating condition and fit for the voyage, and does not ensure the tanks and other spaces in which product is to be carried are in good operating condition.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires commercially reasonable efforts to ensure seaworthiness. The modified text removes the guarantee that pipes, pumps, coils and tanks are in good operating condition. This undermines the implied warranty of seaworthiness, potentially conflicting with maritime law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied warranty of seaworthiness under general maritime law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts the implied warranty of seaworthiness, a fundamental principle of maritime law established in cases like *The Osceola*. While 'commercially reasonable efforts' provided some protection, the removal of guarantees regarding critical equipment like pipes, pumps, and tanks significantly weakens the vessel owner's responsibility to provide a seaworthy vessel, potentially violating this implied warranty and creating liability issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: The Osceola | 189 U.S. 158 (1903) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center The Osceola, 189 U.S. 158 (1903) Overview Opinions Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court The Osceola, 189 U.S. 158 (1903) The Osceola No. 98 Argued December 2, 1902 Decided March 2, 1903 189 U.S. 158 Syllabus 1. The law both in England and America is settled as to the following propositions: (1) That a vessel and her owners are liable, in case a seaman falls sick or is wounded in the service of the ship, to the extent of his maintenance and cure, and to his wages at least so long as the voyage is continued. (2) That the vessel and her owners are, both by English and American law, liable to an indemnity for injuries received by seamen in consequence of the unseaworthiness of the ship or a failure to supply and keep in order the proper appliances appurtenant to such ship. (3) That all the members of the crew, except perhaps the master, are, as between themselves, fellow servants, and hence seamen cannot recover for injuries sustained through the negligence of another member of the crew beyond the expense of their maintenance and cure. (4) That the seaman is not allowed to recover an indemnity for the negligence of the master, or any member of the crew, but is entitled to maintenance and cure whether the injuries were received from negligence or accident. 2. Section 3348, Rev.Stat. of 1898 of Wisconsin, providing that every ship, boat or vessel used in navigating the waters of that state shall be liable for all damages arising from injuries done to persons or property thereby, and that the claim therefor shall constitute a lien upon such ship, boat or vessel, is confined to cases where the damage is done by those in charge of a ship, with the ship as the "offending thing." Cases of damages done on board the ship are not, within the meaning of the act, damages done by the ship. Such statute does not create a lien which can be enforced Page 189 U. S. 159 in rem for injuries received by a seaman by the falling of a gangway, resulting as alleged
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation to ensure seaworthiness by removing guarantees regarding critical equipment. This contradicts the implied warranty of seaworthiness, a fundamental principle of maritime law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing *The Osceola*, states that vessel owners are liable for injuries caused by the unseaworthiness of the ship, reinforcing the legal obligation to maintain a seaworthy vessel and provide proper appliances. The modified text, by removing guarantees, undermines this obligation, creating a potential liability issue. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists because the changed text significantly reduces the owner's responsibility to provide a seaworthy vessel, directly conflicting with established maritime law as outlined in the snippet.
